coredata.dc:title: Chemically clean single-step oxido-reductive synthesis of green luminescent graphene quantum dots as impending electrocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Guin, Jhimli Paul
Guin, Saurav K.
Debnath, Tushar
Ghosh, Hirendra N.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we develop a room-temperature, single-step and chemically clean oxido-reductive top–down method to prepare highly pure graphene quantum dots (GQDs) of size 3.3 ± 0.7 nm by non-contact gamma radiolysis of aqueous dispersion of graphene oxide (GNO). The strong oxidising hydroxyl radicals and reducing nascent hydrogen are in situ generated by gamma radiolysis of water, and these radicals simultaneously react with GNO to produce green photoluminescent GQDs possessing some interesting photophysical and electrochemical properties. Both the steady-state and excited-state photophysics of GQDs suggest the involvement of two prominent energy states, which are deeply rooted with its molecular functionalities. By virtue of this novel synthesis approach, the GQDs possess several core-confined π-units together with edge-functional groups and thus show high intrinsic outer-sphere electrode kinetics to reversible electrochemical benchmark redox mediator (viz. K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]) of sensors through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry. The GQDs synthesised through gamma radiolysis show three-order faster intrinsic heterogeneous electron transfer kinetics than hydrothermally synthesised GQDs for that redox couple. This study is expected to open up a new direction for the chemically clean and sustainable synthesis of pure GQDs as impending electrocatalyst for the development of efficient electrochemical sensors.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Steady-state photophysics of GQDs studied by UV–Vis absorbance and PL spectroscopies
Material characteristics of the GQDs studied by TEM, Raman spectroscopy and XPS
Excited-state photophysics of GQDs studied by femtosecond TA spectroscopy
Electrochemical properties of GQDs studied by the CV and EIS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Ultraviolet–Visible (solid lines) and photoluminescence (solid dots) spectra (at excitation wavelength of 385 nm) of 0.5 mg mL−1 (a) GNO (red), (b) GQDs(10) (wine), (c) GQDs(25) (green) and (d) GQDs(50) (blue). The corresponding photoluminescence is denoted by a′, b′, c′ and d′, respectively. Inset shows an enlarged portion of (a). (B) The digital image of 0.5 mg mL−1 GNO, GQDs(10), GQDs(25) and GQDs(50) under visible (first row) and UV (second row) lights. (C) Photoluminescence spectrum (wine) of 0.5 mg mL−1GQDs(10). The green lines show the deconvoluted luminescence peaks and red lines show the fitted peak sum. (D) Schematic diagrams of different photoluminescence energy levels in GQDs(10). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic representation of the oxido-reductive formation of GQDs from GNO. The path numbers 1–3 show various ways of reduction of some reducible groups of GNO and/or GQDs; the path numbers 5 and 6 show the oxidative transformation and cutting of GNO. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Photoluminescence emission scans (solid lines) of GQDs(25) at excitation wavelength 320 nm (violet), 340 nm (blue), 350 nm (wine), 385 nm (green), 400 nm (pink) and 450 nm (red) and the excitation scans (dotted lines) observed for the corresponding maximum emission at 450 nm (violet), 470 nm (blue), 490 nm (wine), 510 nm (green), 520 nm (pink) and 530 nm (red). (B) Variation in the quantum yield (%) of GQDs(25) as a function of the excitation wavelength. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical absorption spectra of 0.5 mg mL−1 (a) GQDs(25) and (b) GQDs(N2O, 25). Photoluminescence spectra of 0.5 mg mL−1 (a′) GQDs(25) and (b′) GQDs(N2O, 25). Excitation wavelength is 385 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) TEM images with size distribution histogram (based on the statistical analysis over 80 dots) of GQDs(50). (B) HR-TEM images of GQDs(50), where the FFT images of the selected portions are shown beside the selected boxes. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Raman spectra of (A) GNO and (B) GQDs(50) (black lines). The deconvoluted peaks are shown by green lines and the red line represents the fitted sum peak. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

High-resolution XPS spectra (black lines) of C1s and O1s binding energies of (A, B) GNO and (C, D) GQDs(50). The green lines show the deconvoluted peaks and red lines show the fitted sum peaks. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Ultrafast transient absorption (TA) spectrum of GQDs(50) at different time delays from 1 ps to 100 ps after 400 nm laser pulse excitation. (B) TA decay kinetics of ESA1 and ESA2 of GQDs(50) in the presence of DDQ and DDT quencher. (C) Deconvoluted TA spectrum of GQDs(50), GQDs(25) and GQDs(10) at 1 ps time delay. (D) The tentative position of the energy levels involved in ESA1 and ESA2 with respect to the energy levels of DDQ and DDT. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Nyquist plots of 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KNO3 and (B) cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KNO3 at (i) GNO, (ii) GQDs(25) and (iii) GQDs(50). Scan rate (υ) for CV is 50 mV s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (for Nyquist plots) is conducted at E0′ = 0.155 V with an amplitude of 10 mV in the frequency range of 105–10−1 Hz. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) CVs of (i) GQDs(25) and (ii) GQDs(50) in 0.1 M PBS (at pH = 7) in the potential range of 1.0 to −1.85 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. (B) Nyquist plots of 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KNO3 and (C) cyclic voltammograms of 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KNO3 at (i) ERGQDs(25) and (ii) ERGQDs(50). Scan rate (υ) for CV is 50 mV s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (for Nyquist plots) is conducted at E0′ = 0.155 V with an amplitude of 10 mV in the frequency range of 105–10−1 Hz. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Chemically clean single-step oxido-reductive synthesis of green luminescent graphene quantum dots as impending electrocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Radiation Technology Development Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai, 400085, Maharashtra, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fuel Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai, 400085, Maharashtra, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Radiation and Photochemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai, 400085, Maharashtra, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we develop a room-temperature, single-step and chemically clean oxido-reductive top–down method to prepare highly pure graphene quantum dots (GQDs) of size 3.3 ± 0.7 nm by non-contact gamma radiolysis of aqueous dispersion of graphene oxide (GNO). The strong oxidising hydroxyl radicals and reducing nascent hydrogen are in situ generated by gamma radiolysis of water, and these radicals simultaneously react with GNO to produce green photoluminescent GQDs possessing some interesting photophysical and electrochemical properties. Both the steady-state and excited-state photophysics of GQDs suggest the involvement of two prominent energy states, which are deeply rooted with its molecular functionalities. By virtue of this novel synthesis approach, the GQDs possess several core-confined π-units together with edge-functional groups and thus show high intrinsic outer-sphere electrode kinetics to reversible electrochemical benchmark redox mediator (viz. K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]) of sensors through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry. The GQDs synthesised through gamma radiolysis show three-order faster intrinsic heterogeneous electron transfer kinetics than hydrothermally synthesised GQDs for that redox couple. This study is expected to open up a new direction for the chemically clean and sustainable synthesis of pure GQDs as impending electrocatalyst for the development of efficient electrochemical sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene consisting of honeycomb lattice of carbon atoms with sp2 hybridisation has attracted significant research interests since its isolation from highly oriented pyrolytic graphite by Novoselov and Geim using scotch tape in 2004 [1]. Although graphene is a zero band gap semiconductor with an infinite exciton Bohr radius, the change of its electronic property by confining π-electron conjugation within nanoscale dimension was theoretically predicted after 4–5 years [2,3]. Soon after, this prediction was experimentally proved by several scientists [4–7]. The zero-dimensional graphene units of lateral size <100 nm are termed as graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Because of the prominent edge effect in addition to the quantum confinement effects, GQDs are endowed in recent years to extend the research focus to photovoltaic devices, light-emitting diodes, photocatalysts, batteries, chemical sensors, biosensors, bioimaging devices and so on [8–11]. GQDs are physicochemically different from other common inorganic semiconductor and metal quantum dots. The excitation wavelength-dependent multi-luminescence property of GQDs places them into different classes of materials compared to the conventional inorganic quantum dots.

The top–down approach is one of the popular routes to prepare GQDs of tunable size. It involves the decomposition and exfoliation of bulk sp2 carbon materials such as graphene (GN), graphene oxide (GNO), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) by acidic oxidation [12–14], hydrothermal [15–17], solvothermal [18,19], electrochemical [20–27], chemical exfoliation [28,29], microwave-aided [30–32], sonication [33], photo-fenton [34] and ozonolysis [35] methods. Majority of these methods demand high temperature or high electricity. Moreover, the by-products or reagents involved in the conventional synthesis processes of GQDs have high concentration of inorganic salts and acids. Hence, stringent and fussy purification processes such as dialysis and ultra-filtration have become mandatory to obtain chemically pure form of GQDs. Further, all the conventional procedures involve only oxidative cutting of GNO, and hence, the final product becomes highly semiconducting or insulating in nature [36]. Therefore, an additional reduction step is included to prepare reduced and conducting forms of GQDs (rGQDs) applicable in electrochemical devices. Therefore, it is of interest to design a chemically clean (or involving minimum easily removable additives), single-step, oxido-reductive synthesis route to prepare chemically pure GQDs for efficient electrochemical applications.

Gamma radiolysis is predicted as a good option for the synthesis of simple chemically clean (green) room-temperature GQDs through aqueous-based top–down synthesis because of its several advantages over the other conventional chemical methods. First, gamma radiolysis is a simple, room-temperature and well established non-contact civil nuclear technique, which has been routinely operated safely for sterilisation of food and medicines and material processing. It is also used for cancer diagnosis and in certain types of cancer treatments. Second, gamma radiolysis of water generates strong oxidising hydroxyl radical (OH; E0′ = 2.72 V vs. normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) in acidic solution), which can fragment organic supramolecules [37–39] as well as shorten and functionalise CNTs [40–43]. Moreover, during the radiolysis process, strong reducing agents such as hydrogen atom (H; E0′ = −2.3 V vs. NHE) and hydrated electrons (e- aq; E0′ = −2.8 V vs. NHE) are produced, which can effectively reduce GNOs to graphene or reduced GNO [44,45]. The general reaction schemes for the radiolysis of water are given in Supporting Information in Section S1. Therefore, it has all the qualities to emerge as a hazardous-chemical-free green chemical technique for the synthesis of GQDs. During the progress of this study, it was observed that some attempts have been made to enhance the photoluminescence (PL) properties of the electrochemically synthesised or purchased GQDs by gamma radiation [46–48]; however, to the best of our knowledge, no attempt has been made to date to utilise the greener aspect of gamma radiolysis for the synthesis of chemically pure GQDs at room temperature. Hence, in this study, we report the possibility of synthesizing GQDs by simply irradiating aqueous dispersion of GNO with remotely operated gamma rays at room temperature. The primary intention of this synthesis is to realise the OH-mediated oxidative cutting of the GNO sheet into small zero-dimensional carbon dots and to embed more electron-rich centres into those carbon dots by reducing some of their oxygen functionalities by the reaction with H or e- aq. The GQDs are characterised by Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Steady- and excited-state photophysical properties were monitored by ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorbance spectroscopy, PL spectroscopy and femtosecond transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy. The synthesised GQDs possess special photochemical and electrochemical properties, which are attributed to this special route of synthesis. The interaction of two energy states rooting from the chemical functionalities of GQDs has been systematically revealed in the following sections by the steady-state and excited-state photophysics of GQDs. The GQDs synthesised by radiation-based process show three-order faster intrinsic heterogeneous electron transfer kinetics than hydrothermally synthesised GQDs for a benchmark redox couple K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6], which is frequently used as redox mediator in several sensors [49,50]. The results show an impending opportunity to groom up these GQDs as potential electrocatalysts for developing various efficient sensors.

Analytical reagent-grade hydrogen peroxide (50%), potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), 2,3-dichloro-5,6-dicyano-1,4-benzoquinone (DDQ) and 1-dodecanethiol (DDT) were used as received. High-purity nitrous oxide (N2O) was used to purge the solution in an experiment discussed below. Ultrapure water (Milli-Q, Millipore, 18.2 MΩ cm) was used to prepare all solutions. The GNO, purchased from M/s. Global Nanotech with its product certificates, was used without any further purification.

Unless specified, the GQDs are prepared by steady-state gamma radiolysis of 10 mL of 0.5 mg mL−1 aqueous solution of GNO in the presence of 25% H2O2 at different doses using 60Co gamma radiation with a dose rate of 1.0 kGy h−1. The pH of the solution was measured as 2. H2O2 was the only chemical used to increase the chemical yield of the GQDs, and it is an environmentally benign mild oxidant that generates water as the only co-product [51–56]. The dose rate of the gamma source was determined by Fricke dosimetry [G(Fe3+) = 15.6/(100 eV)]. The solution was kept inside a properly lead-shielded gamma chamber through an electronically operated motorised stage, which ensures safety of the operator. The irradiation proceeds in non-contact mode and does not induce any radioactivity in the solution. The gamma chambers were designed conforming to the American National Standard, “Self-contained, dry source storage of gamma irradiator,” ANSI N433.1–1977. After gamma radiolysis of the GNO–H2O2 solution at a specified dose (the time of irradiation is calculated as the quotient of the specified dose and the dose rate), the larger residual GNOs were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. Then, water and H2O2 were separated from the supernatant by rota-evaporation in two consecutive cycles. The solid residue was isolated, quantified and dispersed in the water for a concentration of approximately 9 mg mL−1. The pH of the final GQD dispersion in water was measured as 6.2. The GQD prepared by radiolysis at X kGy dose will be represented by GQD(X) in the subsequent discussion.

U-2800 spectrophotometer and Horiba Canada model no. PTI QM-400 spectrofluorimeter were used to record UV–Vis and luminescence spectra, respectively. EQ781 UV cabinet with UV filter is used to visualise the GQDs. The relative emission quantum yields (φ) of the GQD(X) were measured using standard rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) dye solution. A brief description of the calculation of φ is given in Supporting Information in Section S2. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) observation was carried out by a CARL ZEISS LIBRA 200FE transmission electron microscope. Raman spectra were recorded by Seki's STR300 Raman spectrometer using an excitation wavelength of 532 nm from a fibre-coupled diode-pumped solid-state (DPSS) laser source. SPECS instrument with a PHOBIOS 100/150 delay-line detector was used to carry out XPS by employing Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV).

TA measurements were carried out at 400 nm laser excitation (pump pulses) and probe at visible to near infrared (IR) region in femtosecond time resolution. The excited-state dynamics were measured from 0.1 ps to 4 ns timescale using ExciPro spectrometer (CDP, Moscow, Russia). The seed pulses of 800 nm, 40 fs pulse duration, 100 MHz repetition rate and approximately 4 nJ pulse−1 energy were generated from a self-mode-locked Ti:Sapphire laser oscillator (Tissa 50, CDP, Moscow, Russia). The pulses were amplified in a multi-pass amplifier pumped by a 20 W DPSS laser (Jade-II, Thales Laser, France) to generate 40 fs laser pulses of about 1.2 mJ energy at 1 kHz repetition rate. Finally, the amplified pulses were divided into two beams by a beam splitter. One beam was directly used as pump pulse after passing through frequency doubling β-barium borate (BBO) crystal. The other beam was used to generate white light (probe, from 450 to 900 nm) after focusing on a 3 mm thick sapphire window. Finally, the white light (probe pulses) was split into two beams: signal and reference. The pump pulse was overlapped with the probe pulse on the sample. The samples were rotated to avoid photo-bleaching. Instrument response function (IRF) was measured to be approximately 100 fs using the bleach signal of sodium salt of meso-tetrakis (4 sulphonatophenyl) porphyrin (TPPS) at 710 nm. To prevent multiple charge carriers, we keep <Neh> < 1, where <Neh> = (jpσ a ). The terms N eh , σ a and j
p are referred as the average number of charge carrier excited, absorption cross section of the sample and the pump fluence (cm−2), respectively. All the fitting of kinetics trace were fitted using LabVIEW software [57].

The electrochemical experiments were carried out at room temperature (T = 298 K) in a conventional three-electrode cell (5 mL, single compartment) by using AutoLab30 electrochemical workstations. A glassy carbon (GC) electrode (diameter 3 mm) was polished using alumina slurries with different powders of size <0.05 μm. The electrode was thoroughly rinsed with water and sonicated in ultrapure water in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min to remove any adsorbed substances from the electrode surface. Then, 10 μL of approximately 5 mg mL−1 GQD(X) or GNO dispersion was drop-casted on the GC electrode and dried in air. Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode and a platinum wire acted as the counter-electrode. All the potentials were reported with respect to the Ag/AgCl reference electrode. Prior to conducting the electrochemical experiments, the working solution was purged with high-purity nitrogen for 15 min to remove the dissolved oxygen from the solution. Other specific experimental details are available at the respective parts of the discussion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state photophysics of GQDs studied by UV–Vis absorbance and PL spectroscopies
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesis route of GQDs as presented here is indeed explorative and thus it demands a deeper understanding of its enlightening material characteristics. The research interest begins with its spectroscopic properties. Fig. 1 A shows the UV–Vis absorbance (a) and PL (a′, excited at 385 nm) spectra of aqueous dispersion of 0.5 mg mL−1 GNO. The characteristic peak around 257 nm (Fig. S1A) corresponds to the π–π∗ plasmon peak of GNO, whereas the small humps in the wavelength range of 301–312 nm (inset of Fig. 1A) represent n–π∗ plasmons in the oxygen functionalities of GNO [58,59]. In addition to these localised transitions, the long non-zero absorbance in the wavelength range of 350–750 nm represents the underlying basic band structure of graphene sheet. The inset of Fig. 1A shows better representation of n–π∗ plasmons of GNO. The digital images of aqueous dispersion of GNO under visible and UV illuminations are shown in Fig. 1B. The brown solution of GNO observed under visible light is changed to slightly green luminescent under UV light illumination. The luminescence shows a broad band peaking at 620 nm with a quantum yield (φ) of 0.5% (Fig. S1B). This weak PL in GNO is attributed to the radiative recombination of electron–hole (e–h) pairs, localised within the small clusters of planar π-network of sp2 carbons embedded within a disrupting sp3 matrix comprising phenol hydroxyl, epoxy, carbonyl and carboxyl groups [60,61]. The GQDs prepared by radiolysis at X kGy dose will be termed as GQDs(X) in the subsequent discussions. The absorption edge of the dispersion of GQDs(10) (radiolysis at 10 kGy dose) is drastically shifted towards the longer wavelengths compared with GNO, and the increase of light absorption in the wavelength range of 300–750 nm indicates the appearance of some mid-band energy levels from the restoration of conjugation in the π-network of GNO [14,62,63]. Therefore, the reducing radicals, that is, Hs effectively in situ reduce the oxygen functionalities of GNO to produce GQDs(10) [reaction paths 1–4 in Fig. 2 ] [64]. This is the expected advantage of the simultaneous oxido-reductive gamma radiation-based synthesis route over other existing conventional synthesis methods for GQDs. The average band gap (Eg) of the sp2 clusters of π-network present in GQDs(10) is calculated as 3.1 eV (at λ edge = 393 nm) from Equation (1) [65].
(1)
E g
( eV )
= 1239
. 86 /λ
edge
( nm )
,
where λ edge represents the absorption edge value in nanometres. While excited with a light of wavelength 385 nm (∼3.22 eV), the intensity of PL of GQDs(10) increases (Fig. 1Ab′; φ = 0.9%), but the luminescence peak is shifted towards the shorter wavelength (600 nm) compared with GNO (Fig. 1Aa′; φ = 0.5%). This observation suggests that there is an increase in the number of the oxygen functional groups in the GQDs(10) compared with GNO [reaction paths 5 and 6 in Fig. 2] [46–48]. It is important to mention that the broad luminescence band actually comprised two luminescence bands: a low-intensity band peaking around 517 nm (∼2.4 eV) and a high-intensity band peaking around 603 nm (∼2.1 eV) (Fig. 1C). The former band is originated from the π∗–n and π∗–π radiative transitions in the OH-centres of GQDs(10) and the latter high-intensity band is originated from the carbonyl or carboxyl group centres of GQDs(10) [66]. This phenomenon is schematically shown in Fig. 1D. Therefore, the unique phenomenon observed at the initial stage of gamma irradiation of the aqueous solution of GNO is that the fraction of π-conjugation increases in the product by the reaction of in situ generated H with GNO, but OH content in the product simultaneously increases by the reaction of in situ generated OH with GNO (Fig. 2). This phenomenon has not been proved to date by any of the conventional synthesis routes for GQDs.

On increasing the radiation dose further, the fragmentation of GNO by OH becomes prominent and the absorption edge of GQDs(25) (Fig. 1Ac) is drastically shifted towards the shorter wavelengths compared with GQDs(10) (Fig. 1Ab). The band gap energy of GQDs is also increased from 3.1 eV for GQDs(10) to 3.3 eV for GQDs(25). This indicates that the π-conjugation length of GQDs decreases at higher dose, which is attributed to the fragmentation of GNO by OH. However, the presence of significant absorbance in the wavelength range of 370–600 nm suggests that still several vacant energy states exist below the continuum conduction band edge. Fig. 1B shows that the brown aqueous dispersion of GQDs(10) is quite different from the lighter yellow aqueous dispersion of GQDs(25) under visible light illumination. The green PL of GQDs(25) under UV light becomes more intense than the luminescence of GQDs(10). This is also supported by the increase in the quantum yield of GQDs(25) to 2.4%. The shift of the PL band towards the shorter wavelength (i.e. 573 nm) (Fig. 1Ac′) is attributed to an interplay between the quantum confinement and surface functionalisation of GQDs. Two radiative PL components are also manifested in this broad PL band. (Fig. S2A). The radiative component corresponding to the π∗–n and π∗–π transitions is shifted towards the shorter wavelength (511 nm) and its contribution to the overall PL band also increases (Fig. S2A). On the contrary, contribution of the emissive component originated from the carbonyl or carboxyl group centres to the overall PL band decreases. Moreover, this PL component is also found to be shifted towards the shorter wavelength (594 nm) (Fig. S2A). The GQDs are believed to be different luminescent material from inorganic quantum dots [8–11], and they exhibit special PL behaviour. The luminescence maxima are observed at about 523 (at λ ex = 320 nm), 535 (at λ ex = 340 nm), 543 (at λ ex = 350 nm), 573 (at λ ex = 385 nm), 574 (at λ ex = 400 nm) and 604 nm (at λ ex = 450 nm) for GQDs(25) after exciting at different wavelengths as shown in Fig. 3 A. It is evident that the intensity and the peak position of the luminescence spectra of GQDs(25) strongly depend on the wavelength of excitation (Fig. 3A). The maximum quantum yield is observed in the excitation wavelength range of 375–400 nm and it decreases significantly on either sides of that range (Fig. 3B). It is attributed to the maximum number of density of states for the radiative transitions in these wavelength regions. The excitation spectra for each corresponding luminescence maximum are plotted to understand the multi-luminescence phenomenon of GQDs (Fig. 3A). Characteristic broad absorption states exist for each luminescence spectra. For example, the maximum probable luminescence peaking around 573 nm is initiated from the absorption of light at approximately 380–400 nm by GQDs(25). Therefore, it can be concluded that the GQDs comprise multi-emissive energy states as schematically shown in Fig. S2B [66,67].

At this stage, it is of interest to understand the importance of the presence of reducing radical (H) during the oxidative cutting of GNO in the overall photophysical properties of GQDs. Thus, the effect of H on the absorbance and luminescence properties of GQDs is confirmed by preparing GQDs in N2O saturated solution of 0.5 mg mL−1 GNO and 25% H2O2 after gamma radiolysis at 25 kGy dose. In the presence of N2O gas in the solution, the H is converted into OH by the reaction shown in Equation (2):
(2)
H ·
+
N 2
O →
O
·
H +
N 2

The GQDs prepared by radiolysis with 25 kGy dose in the presence of N2O will be termed as GQDs(N2O, 25) in the subsequent discussions. No shift towards the longer wavelengths is seen in the UV–Vis absorption edge of GQDs(N2O, 25), but the n–π∗ transition representing oxygen functionalities becomes more prominent in GQDs(N2O, 25) (Fig. 4 a) than GQDs(25) (Fig. 4b). Therefore, in the absence of H, the fraction of π-conjugation does not increase in GQDs, rather the fraction of OH content increases in GQDs. It also affects the luminescence property of the GQDs. The PL (excited at 385 nm) quantum yield of GQDs(N2O, 25) (Fig. 4b′) decreases compared with GQDs(25) (Fig. 4a′). The PL band of GQDs(N2O, 25) is also shifted towards the shorter wavelength (∼525 nm) compared with GQDs(25). Therefore, no more N2O is used in the actual synthesis of GQDs. The result shows that the simultaneous oxido-reductive synthesis strategy by employing gamma radiolysis produces unique type of GQDs with the coexistence of π electrons and oxygenated functionalities. This creates a concomitant synergistic PL, which is not evident for any conventional chemical technique involving only a single step. The PL intensity decreases with decreasing H2O2 concentration (Fig. S3A) and increasing GNO concentration (Fig. S3B). In the experiments, the optimised concentrations of GNO and H2O2 are found to be 0.5 mg mL−1 and 25%, respectively. The addition of H2O2 increases the chemical yield of the GQDs by increasing the yield of OH radical during the radiolysis process. The excess H2O2 has been easily removed from the GQDs by rota-evaporation.

The absorption edge of GQDs is further shifted towards the shorter wavelengths with increasing the radiation dose from 25 (Fig. 1Ac) to 50 kGy (Fig. 1Ad). This observation suggests that the band gap of GQDs(50) also increases from 3.3 to 3.5 eV with doses. A pale yellow solution of GQDs(50) under visible light becomes a lighter green luminescent solution under UV light illumination (Fig. 1B). The quantum yield (φ) of GQDs(50) increases to 7.4% and the peak of the PL band is shifted towards shorter wavelengths (532 nm) (Fig. 1Ad). The relative contribution of the π∗–n and π∗–π radiative transitions increases in the overall PL band and the peak position of this radiative component is shifted slightly to shorter wavelength (501 nm). On the contrary, the relative contribution of the radiative transitions involving carbonyl or carboxyl group centres decreases in the overall PL band, and the peak position of this radiative component is also shifted to shorter wavelength (572 nm) (Fig. S4A). It is interesting to see that both the excitation and PL spectra of GQDs(50) are shifted to shorter wavelengths (i.e. 340 and 532 nm, respectively) compared with GQDs(25) (Fig. S4A), which can be attributed to the widening of the energy levels involved in the radiative transition because of the quantum confinement effect in GQDs. This is schematically represented in Fig. S4B. By contrast, the equivalent amount of GNO exposed in only 25% H2O2 for 50 h (which is the equivalent time to the radiolysis of 50 kGy dose at the rate of 1 kGy h−1) showed very low intense PL with φ = 0.67%. The digital images of the same under (a) visible and (b) UV lights are shown in Fig. S5. Therefore, very small lateral size together with high OH content as well as associated π-networks can be expected from as-synthesised GQDs(50) through the present oxido-reductive method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characteristics of the GQDs studied by TEM, Raman spectroscopy and XPS
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image shows that the GNO sheet (Fig. S6) is fragmented into monodispersed GQDs(50) with size of 3.3 ± 0.7 nm (based on the statistical analysis over 80 dots) (Fig. 5 A). Fig. 5B shows the HR-TEM images of individual GQD having good crystallinity with in-plane lattice constant of 0.17 nm, which is much smaller than that of graphite (i.e. 0.24 nm) [68]. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns of selected GQDs(50) (as shown by square boxes in Fig. 5B) show clear hexagonal diffraction pattern of single-layer graphene (green box) [69] and turbostratic stacking of two layers of graphene (orange box). Therefore, it can be concluded that the ensembles of GQDs(50) are a mixture of mono- and bi-layer graphene(s). However, under present circumstances, it is difficult to determine the ratio of these two types in the GQD ensemble [70].

GNO shows three characteristic Raman peaks at 1361 (D), 1430 (A) and 1576 cm−1 (G) (Fig. 6 A). The peak at 1361 cm−1 represents the D-band of GNO, and it is attributed to the transverse optical phonon activated from the defect site around K-point of the Brillouin zone [71,72]. The in-plane vibrations (E2g mode) of the graphitic structure resulting from all pairs of sp2 atoms in both rings and chains lead to the Raman peak at 1576 cm−1, which is known as G-band. The small peak at 1430 cm−1 represents the stretching frequency of individual alkene unit of GNO. The intensity ratio of D-band to G-band (ID/IG) of GNO is calculated to be 0.694, whereas that of individual alkene unit to G-band (IA/IG) of GNO is determined as 0.244. Therefore, it can be confirmed that the basic conjugated graphitic unit is more predominant in GNO than in the individual alkene unit. The ID/IG ratio is increased to 0.816, while the IA/IG ratio is increased to 1.06 in GQDs(50) compared with GNO (Fig. 6B). It suggests that during radiolysis of GNO, the defect sites (i.e. sp3 carbon units or voids) are increased by virtue of the oxidative scissoring of the graphitic unit by OH, but at the same time, reduction by H helps to introduce some alkene units in the nanoscale quantum dots (Fig. 2).

The functional groups of GQDs(50) are probed by XPS (Fig. 7 ). The XPS survey spectra of GNO and GQDs(50) show the characteristic C1s and O1s peaks at 284.4 and 531.4 eV, respectively. The O/C atomic ratio is calculated as 1.23 and 3.69 for GNO and GQDs(50), respectively, from the relative areas under O1s and C1s peaks. This indicates the abundance of oxygenated functional groups as well as the depletion of carbon content in GQDs(50) compared with GNO [37–39] and it complements the results of the Raman spectroscopy as discussed earlier. Fig. 7A shows high-resolution well-fitted C1s spectrum of GNO, which can be deconvoluted into two major peaks. The peaks located at 285.0 and 287.0 eV correspond to the specific binding energy of CC/CC and COC/COH, respectively [73]. The contributions of the deconvoluted peaks are calculated as about 29% (285.0 eV) and 71% (287.0 eV). The well-fitted O1s XPS spectrum of GNO shows only a single peak at 532.0 eV representing COC or CO group (Fig. 7B). The relative contribution of the COC/COH functionality becomes about 61% compared with 37% from the CC/CC functionality in the deconvoluted peaks of high-resolution well-fitted C1s spectrum of GQDs(50) (Fig. 7C). Similarly, an additional deconvoluted peak appeared at 233.2 eV of the well-fitted O1s XPS spectrum of GQDs(50), which represents the relative increase in the OH functionality in the GQDs(50) compared with GNO (Fig. 7D) [15]. It is well correlated with the fate of oxido-reductive productions of GQDs. Therefore, GQDs of lateral dimension <10 nm having enhanced surface functionality, internal voids and core-π-units are prepared by a simple, room-temperature, chemically clean technology based on gamma radiolysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excited-state photophysics of GQDs studied by femtosecond TA spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The excited-state charge relaxation and charge transfer processes in GQDs have been explored by using femtosecond TA spectroscopy through exciting GQDs with 400 nm laser light (pump) and monitoring the change in absorption (ΔOD) of the transients over a broad-band spectral window (450–750 nm). The TA spectrum of GQDs(50) shows two prominent features (Fig. 8 A): (1) a ground-state bleach (GSB) below 500 nm representing depletion of electrons in the ground state due to the transition of electrons from the ground state to about 3.1 eV higher arbitrary energy level by the action of pump laser (400 nm); (2) excited-state absorptions (ESAs) of the electrons from different excited states to higher-energy states by the action of probe pulse. The broad ESA of GQDs(50) recorded at different delay times consists of a hump at approximately 550 nm (ESA1; 2.25 eV) with a maximum around 660 nm (ESA2; 1.88 eV), which clearly indicates the presence of two different types of excited states (Fig. 8A).

Since this is special type of GQDs, it is important to understand the photo-physical nature of these excited states. The transient kinetics of these ESAs are monitored at 550 and 660 nm respectively. Fig. 8B shows the decay kinetics of ESA1 (wine colour) and ESA2 (green) (also see Fig. S7A). Kinetics at 550 nm (for ESA1) has been best-fitted with bi-exponential growth of τ g 1 = 100 (80%) and τ g 2 = 400 fs (20%) and multi-exponential decay with time constants of τ1 = 4 ps (48%), τ2 = 25 ps (24%), τ3 = 200 ps (14%) and τ4 ≥ 1 ns (14%). On the contrary, kinetics at 660 nm (ESA2) can be fitted with pulse-width-limited single exponential growth (<100 fs) followed by multi-exponential decay with time constants of τ1 = 2 ps (41%), τ2 = 25 ps (35%), τ3 = 200 ps (11%) and τ4 ≥ 1 ns (13%). Here, single exponential growth of 100 fs in ESA2 indicates immediate population of the corresponding state after laser pulse excitation, whereas the second growth component of 400 fs in ESA1 implies electron cooling time to the corresponding state. This observation clearly shows that ESA1 involves lower energetic excited state, whereas ESA2 involves higher energetic state. To obtain more insight of these ESAs, we have compared the ESA signals of GQDs(10), GQDs(25) and GQDs(50) samples at 1 ps delay time after deconvolution into two ESA peaks (Fig. 8C). It is also observed that the position of ESA1 does not appreciably change with the size of GQDs, but ESA2 moves towards the shorter wavelengths with decreasing the size of GQDs. By recalling the steady-state luminescence characteristics of the GQDs, we found that the bluer component of the green luminescence did not much move to shorter wavelength, but the redder component of the green luminescence moved significantly to shorter wavelength with decreasing the size of the GQDs. Therefore, ESAs well complement these steady-state emissive states (Fig. 8D). Now, to unravel the origin of these emissive states, quenching experiment is carried out after addition of both electron quencher (2,3-dichloro-5,6-dicyano-1,4-benzoquinone, DDQ; E0 = −5.2 eV vs. vacuum) and hole quencher (1-dodecanethiol, DDT; E0 = −5.7 eV vs. vacuum) in GQDs(50) (Fig. 8B). Although the decay kinetics of both ESA1 and ESA2 becomes faster in the presence of hole acceptor (DDT), the decay trace of ESA1 becomes faster only in the presence of electron quencher (DDQ). To demonstrate both electron- and hole-quenching processes, a scheme has been drawn, and the ground and excited energy levels of GQDs(50), DDT and DDQ are shown in Fig. 8D. It is clear from the scheme that the hole transfer process for photo-excited GQDs(50) (responsible for both ESA1 and ESA 2) to DDT is a thermodynamically viable process. Again the scheme suggests that electron transfer from both ESA2 and ESA1 to DDQ is thermodynamically viable. However, it is interesting to see that electron transfer only occurs for ESA1. In an earlier section from steady-state luminescence measurements, we have mentioned that the particular energy levels (involved in ESA1) are originated from the edge-built state (ES) OH groups situated at the edges and near any π-conjugation (like carbonyl and/or alkene group), and it appreciably depends on the size of the GQDs [74–76]. Thus, the π-conjugated electrons at ES can easily be quenched by DDQ. By decreasing the size of the GQDs, the ES is energetically uplifted to a similar extent as compared to the higher energy levels. Thus, the peak position of ESA1 does not change much with the size of the GQDs. On the contrary, the energy states involving ESA2 are originated from the deep-built (DS) energy levels from the OH groups decorated on the basal planes of GQDs and it does not depend appreciably on the size of the GQDs. Therefore, the peak position of ESA2 is shifted towards the higher-energy side with decreasing the size of the GQDs. It is also clear from electron quenching experiments that electron transfer from DS state (responsible for ESA2) to DDQ does not occur.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of GQDs studied by the CV and EIS
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs have received some attention in both fundamental and applied electrochemistry because of its semiconducting band gap and surface functionality (i.e. surface states) [27,65,77–81]. Although GQDs prepared by hydrothermal methods showed catalytic electron transfer activity in some small organic molecules because of its surface functionality [79], the outer-sphere heterogeneous electron transfer kinetics of GQDs is not expected to be encouraging as electrocatalyst as compared to electrochemically reduced GNO [36,82–84]. The GQDs prepared by gamma radiolysis have already revealed synergistic electronic interaction with the electron and hole quenchers through surface functionality and core-π-units. Therefore, it is of interest to understand the intrinsic electrochemical properties of GQDs in terms of heterogeneous outer-sphere electron transfer reactions by probing with a benchmark electrochemical redox couple, namely K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6], which is often used as mediators for electrochemical sensors.

Fig. 9A(i) shows the Nyquist plot of GNO in 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KNO3 at a potential of 0.155 V with an amplitude of 10 mV in the frequency range of 105–10−1 Hz. The charge transfer resistance of GNO is calculated as 31,625 Ω by fitting with an electrical equivalent circuit R1(R2Q1), where R1 is the solution resistance, R2 is the charge transfer resistance, Q1 is the constant phase element accounting for the electrode roughness and the parentheses represent the parallel circuit arrangement between R2 and Q1. The charge transfer resistance significantly decreases to 745 Ω at GQDs(25) (Fig. 9 A(ii)), indicating that GQDs are facilitating the electron transfer kinetics at the electrode–electrolyte interface, thereby catalysing the outer-sphere electron transfer rate of the probe redox couple. The charge transfer resistance further decreases to 453 Ω for GQDs(50) (Fig. 9A(iii)), which indicates further facilitation in the electron transfer kinetics of the probe by GQDs(50). The Nyquist plots of K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] on GQDs(25) and GQDs(50) are fitted with an electrical equivalent circuit R1(Q1[R2W]), where W represents the Warburg impedance (accounting for the concentration polarisation) and the parentheses represent the serial circuit arrangement between R2 and W. The concentration polarisation impedance is represented in the plot by the linear portion just after the semicircular part, which becomes prominent at lower frequencies. Therefore, EIS study reveals that the electron transfer property of GQD is better than that of GNO. The electron transfer rate is increased with decrease in the size as well as increase in the oxygen functionality of GQDs [80]. This is attributed to the increased proportion of the reactive edge structures in basal plane structures in GQDs with decrease in the size. Moreover, the capacitance, which is indirectly represented by the highest observed imaginary component of the impedance (–Z″ max), decreases from GNO to GQD(50) because of the enhancement of the oxygenated functionalities, and this result is well supported by the observation of Liu et al. [80].

Fig. 9B shows the cyclic voltammograms of 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KNO3 at a scan rate (υ) of 50 mV s−1 on (i) GNO, (ii) GQDs(25) and (iii) GQDs(50). The peak currents for reduction of K3[Fe(CN)6] to K4[Fe(CN)6] in the forward potential scan and oxidation of K4[Fe(CN)6] to K3[Fe(CN)6] in the reverse potential scan systematically increase from GNO to GQDs(50), although the loading of two-dimensional GNO and/or GQDs on the base electrode is the same in this comparison. Therefore, the interfacial electron transfer kinetics becomes faster with the decrease of size and increase of functionalities (both oxygenated and π-electrons), but a reverse trend is observed in the variation of the peak potentials due to the complicated involvement of quantum confinement effects with the surface functionalities. This phenomenon is well supported by the EIS studies as discussed in the previous paragraph. The slopes of the straight lines of the plots ln(cathodic peak current) versus Ln(scan rate) are calculated as 0.3 (R2 = 0.995) for GNO, 0.29 (R2 = 0.997) for GQDs(25) and 0.36 (R2 = 0.997) for GQDs(50) (Insets of Fig. S8(A–C)). It represents that the electron transfer reaction of K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] redox probe at the studied electrodes is controlled by the diffusion-controlled mass transfer process. We assume that the diffusion coefficients (D) of K3[Fe(CN)6] and K4[Fe(CN)6] are equal, that is, 6.73 × 10−6 cm2 s−1. The electron transfer rate constants of K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] redox couple on GNO, GQDs(25) and GQDs(50) are calculated by Equations (3) and (4) [82–84].
(3)
Ψ =
k 0
[
nπFDυ
RT
]
1 2
where
(4)
Ψ =
1
35.87169 Δ
E p
− 2.0999
and n, k0 and ΔEp are the number of electron transfer (here n = 1), the standard rate constant of the reduction of K3[Fe(CN)6] and the difference between the cathodic and anodic peak potentials of cyclic voltammograms recorded at different scan rates ranging from 25 to 300 mV s−1 (Fig. S8(A–C)). The value of k0 increases from 4.87 × 10−4 cm s−1 at GNO to 1.26 × 10−3 cm s−1 at GQDs(25) and further to 1.52 × 10−3 cm s−1 at GQDs(50). This suggests that GQDs are capable enough of catalysing the electron mediation between the redox probe and the electrode. This is attributed to the presence of alkene units (in this synthesis condition) and other oxygenated surface functional groups in GQDs. The rate of this reaction was observed to be three orders faster than that of the same reaction on the hydrothermally prepared GQDs [79]. It can be inferred that the radiolytically synthesised GQDs show much superior outer-sphere electron transfer ability to chemically prepared GQDs, which is attributed to the introduction of some unique features into the radiolytically synthesised GQDs during simultaneous oxido-reductive synthesis of GQDs at room temperature through gamma radiolysis. Fig. 10 A shows the cyclic voltammograms of (i) GQDs(25) and (ii) GQDs(50) in 0.1 M PBS (at pH = 7) in the potential range of 1.0 to −1.85 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The cathodic peak starting from about −1.35 V represents the electrochemical reduction of the residual surface-bound peroxyl, aldehyde and epoxy groups on GQDs. The apparent amount of the reducible groups (n, in μmol g−1) of GQDs was calculated from the cathodic peaks by using Faraday's law of electrolysis (Equation (5)):
(5)
N
(
μmol
g
− 1
)
=
Q
( μC )
/
[ zF
(
C
mol
− 1
)
W
( g )
]
,
where Q (in μC) is the integrated charge corresponding to the cathodic peak; z is the number of electrons exchanged for the reduction of each peroxyl, aldehyde and epoxy groups (here, z = 2); F is Faraday's constant (96,485 C mol−1) and W (in g) is the weight of GQD casted on the GC electrode. The amounts of residual reducible groups in GQDs(25) and GQDs(50) are calculated as 4.2 and 1.8 μmol g−1, respectively. This supports the effectiveness of the oxido-reductive radiolysis technique to prepare GQDs having higher electron transfer efficiency. The electrochemically reduced GQDs(25) and GQDs(50) will be termed in the following paragraphs as ERGQDs(25) and ERGQDs(50), respectively. Fig. 10B shows the Nyquist plots of (i) ERGQDs(25) and (ii) ERGQDs(50) for the same redox couples. The charge transfer resistance slightly decreases to 693 Ω at ERGQDs(25) compared to 745 Ω at GQDs(25), whereas it significantly decreases to 149 Ω at ERGQDs(50) compared to 453 Ω at GQDs(50). This is attributed to the restoration of π-conjugations at ERGQDs as compared to GQDs. Therefore, the ERGQDs(50) is seemed to be the best heterogeneous electron transfer kinetics among the studied materials. Fig. 10C shows the cyclic voltammograms of 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KNO3 at a scan rate (υ) of 50 mV s−1 on (i) ERGQDs(25) and (ii) ERGQDs(50). Both the cathodic and anodic peak currents increase for this redox reaction at ERGQDs(50) compared to ERGQDs(25). Further, the peak-to-peak separation is calculated as 0.094 V for ERGQDs(50) compared to 0.145 V for ERGQDs(25) and 0.155 V for GQDs(50). Therefore, the electrochemical reversibility is increased at ERGQDs(50) as compared to GQDs(50). The k0 of the heterogeneous electron transfer reaction for ERGQD(25) and ERGQD(50) was calculated from Equations (3) and (4) as 1.2 × 10−3 and 2.69 × 10−3 cm s−1, respectively, by varying the scan rate from 25 to 300 mV s−1 (Fig. S9(A,B)). Thus, ERGQD is expected to become an efficient electrocatalyst for various electrochemical applications in the near future.

It can be concluded that non-contact gamma radiolysis of aqueous dispersion of GNO at room temperature opens up a novel single-step oxido-reductive green chemical synthesis route for the controlled synthesis of GQDs, and it reduces the consumption and production of hazardous chemicals. Furthermore, to date, the radiolytically synthesised GQDs have shown excellent photo-physical and electrochemical properties compared to chemically prepared GQDs. The GQDs synthesised by radiolysis have balanced mass of oxygenated surface and basal functionalities as well as several core-confined π-units. It is one of the promising achievements for any single-step synthesis of GQDs at room temperature. Under excitation with UV lights, these GQDs show green PL involving multi-emissive energy levels rooting at various core and surface states of GQDs. Furthermore, the steady-state PL as well as the ESA studies collectively reveal that the green PL of radiolytically synthesised GQDs involves a basal plane isolated oxygen functionality and edge-plane oxygen functionalities in conjugation with graphitic π-units. The edge functionalities as well as the basal graphitic π-units facilitate the outer-sphere heterogeneous electron transfer kinetic of a bench-mark redox mediator, which could be used for various electrochemical applications. Thus, this study opens up a new direction for the green chemical synthesis of GQDs as impending electrocatalyst for the development of efficient electrochemical sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely acknowledge Dr. Suman Neogy, Materials Science Division; Dr. K. Bhattacharyya, Chemistry Division, and Dr. Apurav Guleria, Radiation and Photochemistry Division, for TEM, XPS and Raman Spectroscopy studies, respectively. Dr. Saurav K. Guin acknowledges Mr. Arvind S. Ambolikar for his kind support during the electrochemical experiments. The authors also acknowledge Dr. Y. K. Bhardwaj, Dr. Lalit Varshney, Dr. J. V. Kamat and Dr. S. Kannan for their kind encouragements in this study. Dr. D. Goswami is acknowledged for his help in the rota-evaporation. Tushar Debnath acknowledges CSIR for research fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

It contains information related to (1) generation of strong oxidising hydroxyl radical (OH) and strong reducing nascent hydrogen atom (H) during gamma radiolysis of water; (2) calculation of the photoluminescence quantum yield (φ); (3) UV–Visible and photoluminescence spectra of GNO; (4) photoluminescence spectra of GQDs(25) along with the schematic representation of multiple photophysical energy states; (5) optimisation of the concentrations of H2O2 and GNO; (6) photoluminescence spectra of GQDs(50) along with the schematic representation of photophysical energy states relative to GQDs(25); (7) effect of H2O2 alone on GNO at room temperature; (8) transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image of graphene oxide (GNO); (9) transient absorption spectroscopy of GQDs; (10) cyclic voltammetry of 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KNO3 at GNO, GQDs(25) and GQDs(50) at different scan rates and (11) cyclic voltammetry of 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KNO3 at ERGQDs(25) and ERGQDs(50) at different scan rates.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.08.039.
